summary: Russian-American author and one of the most frequently clued literary figures. Known for ornate prose, unreliable narrators, and wordplay. His most famous novel is Lolita, about pedophile Humbert Humbert's obsession with 12-year-old Dolores Haze. Pale Fire is structured as a 999-line poem by fictional poet John Shade with commentary by Charles Kinbote. Also known for his literal translation of Pushkin's Eugene Onegin (which sparked a feud with Edmund Wilson), the memoir Speak, Memory, and the academic satire Pnin. A lepidopterist, chess enthusiast, and Cornell professor whose wife Vera managed his academic duties.

works.name: Lolita
works.description: Nabokov's best-known novel (1955), narrated by the unreliable Humbert Humbert, a pedophile obsessed with the 12-year-old 'nymphet' Dolores Haze (Lolita). Humbert's childhood love Annabel died of typhus, named for Poe's 'Annabel Lee.' The playwright Clare Quilty pursues Lolita and is eventually murdered by Humbert. A poem in the novel calls Lolita 'Wanted, wanted' and ends 'the rest is rust and stardust.' Humbert hands over a death sentence resembling T.S. Eliot's 'Ash Wednesday' before shooting Quilty. Stanley Kubrick adapted it as a 1962 film; Nabokov wrote a 400-page screenplay that Kubrick heavily edited to conform to the Hays Code. The poster depicts heart-shaped sunglasses. Vera Nabokov stopped her husband from burning the manuscript on several occasions. Azar Nafisi's memoir is titled 'Reading Lolita in Tehran.' The poem 'Lilith' is sometimes read as a precursor. Nabokov anagrammed himself as Vivian Darkbloom within the novel. Wikipedia

works.name: Pale Fire
works.description: A novel structured as a 999-line poem by the fictional poet John Shade, with a foreword and commentary by the unreliable narrator Charles Kinbote, who claims to be the exiled King Charles II of Zembla ('The Beloved'). The poem opens 'I was the shadow of the waxwing slain' (inspired by a Robert Frost image of an owl banking past window glass). Kinbote argues the unwritten thousandth line mirrors the first. Shade is shot by the supposedly insane assassin Jack Grey, who was actually aiming for a former king. Shade's ugly daughter Hazel (daughter of Sybil) drowns herself in an icy lake after an unsuccessful date with Pete Dean. Shade plays chess with his wife after Hazel's drowning. Kinbote and Shade play 'word golf' (going from 'live' to 'dead' in five). The imprisonment of 'The Beloved' is likened to a solus rex chess puzzle. Charles Botkin teaches at Wordsmith College. Wikipedia

works.name: Eugene Onegin Translation / Notes on Prosody
works.description: Nabokov's extremely literal, unrhymed translation of Pushkin's Eugene Onegin, which sparked a famous feud with his erstwhile friend Edmund Wilson. Wilson criticized the translation for 'a lack of common sense.' Nabokov removed the masculine and feminine rhymes from each of the 14-line stanzas. Accompanied by the essay 'Notes on Prosody,' an analysis of Russian and English verse. In Notes on Prosody, Nabokov writes about elision in English versus Russian. He also harshly criticized Walter Arndt's earlier translation. John Barth compared recomposing Beethoven's Sixth Symphony to Nabokov's translation in 'The Literature of Exhaustion.' Wikipedia

works.name: Speak, Memory
works.description: Nabokov's autobiography/memoir, subtitled 'An Autobiography Revisited.' Opens with 'The cradle rocks above an abyss.' Discusses his Swiss-French governess Mademoiselle O (chapter 5), an old fat swan, his love of butterfly-collecting (lepidoptery), and his synesthesia. The last chapter is a second-person address to his wife Vera. Also discusses his mother. Wikipedia

works.name: Pnin
works.description: A novel about Timofey Pnin, a Russian emigre professor at the fictional Waindell College, baffled by 'unpredictable America.' He boards the wrong train for a lecture. He gives a football to the drawing savant Victor, the son of his ex-wife. Squirrels frequently appear as portents of misfortune; his former lover Mira Belochkin (whose last name means 'squirrel') died at Buchenwald. His colleague Leonard Blorenge reads and speaks no French despite being Chair of French Literature. The narrator is V. who insults Pnin. Pnin loses his luggage with his papers while giving a guest lecture. Wikipedia

works.name: "Signs and Symbols"
works.description: One of Nabokov's most anthologized short stories. An elderly couple's institutionalized son suffers from 'referential mania' — he thinks 'everything is a cipher and of everything he is the theme.' The couple tries to bring fruit jellies to their son at a sanatorium. The story ends with an unnamed phone ominously ringing a third time after two misdials from a girl asking for 'Charlie.' The couple reads the labels of jelly jars as the phone rings. Nabokov implied there were 'two plots' in the story. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Boy suffers from 'referential mania' — thinks everything in the world relates back to him

works.name: "The Vane Sisters"
works.description: A short story in which the narrator catches sight of a shadowless icicle and a red-shadowed parking meter. The last paragraph contains an acrostic revealing that the sights were caused by the ghosts of Cynthia and Sybil Vane. Sybil commits suicide; Cynthia devotes herself to spiritualism after Sybil's death. Sybil writes 'Death was not better than D minus' in a French exam paper. The narrator connects first letters of words in Shakespeare's sonnets. Cynthia realizes the name 'Alph' from Kubla Khan links to Anna Livia Plurabelle's initials. Nabokov had to explain the acrostic to the editor of The New Yorker after they rejected it. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Acrostic in final paragraph reveals ghosts of Cynthia and Sybil Vane; icicles and parking meter

works.name: "Spring in Fialta"
works.description: A short story in which the narrator Victor relates memories of a fleeting love affair with Nina, whom he encounters after several years in a Mediterranean resort town. Victor declines to join Nina and her husband Ferdinand for a car journey; they die in an accident with a circus truck. Circus posters constantly reappear throughout the story, prefiguring the death. Collected in Nabokov's Dozen. Wikipedia

works.name: The Defense (The Luzhin Defense)
works.description: An early Nabokov novel about the chess player Luzhin, who is mentored by the ruthless Valentinov. Culminates in a disastrous chess match against the Italian grandmaster Turati, after which Luzhin jumps out of a 'frosted window.' Nabokov worked his interest in chess into the character. Wikipedia

The protagonist is mentored by the ruthless Valentinov

works.name: Ada, or Ardor
works.description: Set on the planet of Anti-Terra (Demonia), where siblings Van Veen and Ada fall in love. Two siblings play a wordplay-heavy game of Scrabble called 'Flavita' (an anagram). Endnotes by Vivian Darkbloom explain punning terms from the provinces of Estoty and Canady. A novel opening parodies Tolstoy: 'all happy families are more or less dissimilar; all unhappy ones are more or less alike.' Wikipedia

clues.clue: Set on Anti-Terra/Demonia; Van Veen falls in love with his sister Ada
examples: The planet of Anti-Terra provides the setting to a novel in which Van Veen falls in love with his sister
two siblings play a wordplay-heavy game of scrabble called 'Flavita'

clues.clue: Parodies Tolstoy: 'all happy families are more or less dissimilar'

works.name: Lectures on Literature / Lectures on Russian Literature
works.description: Collections of Nabokov's university lectures. Lectures on Literature includes maps of Leopold Bloom's Dublin and sketches of the beetle Gregor Samsa. Nabokov urged readers to create detailed maps of Mansfield Park and Dr. Jekyll's London. He termed Austen's technique with Fanny Price the 'knight's move' and stressed the 'play theme' in Mansfield Park. He dismissed 'bad readers' who use fiction to learn about 'life in gay Paree or in sad Russia.' He consistently rated Tolstoy as great, Turgenev as 'pleasant,' and Dostoevsky as 'mediocre,' though he praised The Double. In Lectures on Russian Literature, he both praised and criticized Chekhov. He argued Gregor Samsa was 'merely a big beetle,' not a cockroach. He created a count of 'Victories and Defeats' in Don Quixote lectures to refute Krutch's claim. Wikipedia

works.name: Other Works
works.description: Several other works appear in clues less frequently. Bend Sinister is a dystopian novel set in a land ruled by the 'Party of the Average Man,' featuring references to 'the left.' King, Queen, Knave is an early novel. The Real Life of Sebastian Knight was published while Nabokov taught at Wellesley. 'The Admiralty Spire' is a story consisting of a letter to a 'lady novelist' accusing her of fictionalizing the narrator's first love. 'The Dragon' features a fearsome creature used as an advertising marquee for tobacco companies. 'The Potato Elf' features circus performer Fred Dobson ridiculed for dwarfism. 'Razor' features a Russian officer turned barber who realizes a customer once tortured him. Strong Opinions collects his essays; The Gift is mentioned as another notable novel.

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: Nabokov is identified as a Russian-American author. He was a lepidopterist (butterfly collector), chess enthusiast, and synesthete. He taught at Cornell University (where Thomas Pynchon was his student) and at Wellesley College. His wife Vera handled his proctoring and office hours. Roman Jakobson resisted his appointment at Harvard, saying 'are we next to invite an elephant to be Professor of Zoology?' He quizzed students on traits of 'good' vs. 'bad' readers. He published early works under the pen name V. Sirin. His favorite word is 'mauve' (according to Ben Blatt). He lived at the Montreux Palace Hotel in Switzerland. He inspired postmodern writers including Don DeLillo. W.G. Sebald was influenced by him (appears in The Emigrants). He asked John Wayne 'what do you do for a living?' at a party.

comprehensive_summary:
Vladimir Nabokov is a Russian-American author and one of the most frequently clued literary figures in quizbowl, appearing across difficulties 7 through 10. His two most important works for quiz purposes are Lolita and Pale Fire, which between them account for the majority of giveaway and mid-level clues.

Lolita (1955) is Nabokov's best-known novel, narrated by the unreliable pedophile Humbert Humbert, who is obsessed with the twelve-year-old 'nymphet' Dolores Haze. Humbert's childhood love Annabel died of typhus and is named for Poe's 'Annabel Lee.' The playwright Clare Quilty pursues and kidnaps Lolita; Humbert ultimately murders Quilty after handing him a death sentence resembling T.S. Eliot's 'Ash Wednesday.' A poem within the novel calls Lolita 'Wanted, wanted' and ends with 'the rest is rust and stardust.' Stanley Kubrick adapted it as a 1962 film, for which Nabokov wrote a 400-page screenplay that was heavily edited to conform to the Hays Code. The film poster famously depicts heart-shaped sunglasses. Nabokov anagrammed himself as Vivian Darkbloom within the novel. Vera Nabokov stopped her husband from burning the manuscript on several occasions.

Pale Fire is structured as a 999-line poem by the fictional poet John Shade, with commentary by the unreliable narrator Charles Kinbote, who claims to be the exiled King Charles II of Zembla ('The Beloved'). The poem opens with 'I was the shadow of the waxwing slain,' an image inspired by Robert Frost's poem 'Questioning Faces.' Kinbote argues the unwritten thousandth line would have mirrored the first. Shade is shot by the assassin Jack Grey, who was actually aiming for a former king. Shade's daughter Hazel (daughter of Sybil) drowns in an icy lake after an unsuccessful date with Pete Dean. Shade plays chess with his wife afterward. Kinbote and Shade play 'word golf,' going from 'live' to 'dead' in five steps. The imprisonment of 'The Beloved' is likened to a solus rex chess puzzle. Charles Botkin teaches at Wordsmith College.

Nabokov's extremely literal translation of Pushkin's Eugene Onegin, which removed the masculine and feminine rhymes from each 14-line stanza, sparked a famous feud with his erstwhile friend Edmund Wilson. The accompanying essay 'Notes on Prosody' analyzes Russian and English verse and discusses elision. Nabokov also harshly criticized Walter Arndt's earlier translation.

Speak, Memory is Nabokov's autobiography. It opens with 'The cradle rocks above an abyss' and discusses his governess Mademoiselle O (an old fat swan presages her death), his passion for butterfly-collecting, and his synesthesia. The final chapter is a second-person address to his wife Vera.

Pnin follows the Russian emigre professor Timofey Pnin at fictional Waindell College, baffled by 'unpredictable America.' He boards the wrong train for a lecture, gives a football to the drawing savant Victor (son of his ex-wife), and is confused between football and soccer. Squirrels appear as portents of misfortune throughout, linked to his former lover Mira Belochkin ('squirrel' in Russian) who died at Buchenwald. His colleague Leonard Blorenge reads no French despite being Chair of French Literature.

'Signs and Symbols' is one of Nabokov's most anthologized stories. An elderly couple's institutionalized son suffers from 'referential mania,' thinking everything in the world relates to him. The story ends with a phone ominously ringing a third time, and Nabokov implied the story contains 'two plots.'

'The Vane Sisters' features an acrostic hidden in the final paragraph, revealed by the first letters of each word, which spells a message from the ghosts of Cynthia and Sybil Vane involving icicles and a parking meter. Nabokov had to explain the acrostic to The New Yorker editor after they rejected the story.

'Spring in Fialta' follows narrator Victor's memories of a love affair with Nina at a Mediterranean resort, with circus posters throughout prefiguring Nina's death in a car crash with a circus truck.

The Defense (The Luzhin Defense) follows chess player Luzhin, mentored by the ruthless Valentinov, through a disastrous match against Italian grandmaster Turati, after which Luzhin jumps from a 'frosted window.'

Ada, or Ardor is set on the planet Anti-Terra (Demonia), where siblings Van Veen and Ada fall in love. It opens with a parody of Tolstoy: 'all happy families are more or less dissimilar.' Characters play a Scrabble-like game called 'Flavita,' and endnotes are by Vivian Darkbloom.

In his Lectures on Literature, Nabokov includes maps of Leopold Bloom's Dublin and sketches of Gregor Samsa as a beetle (arguing he is 'merely a big beetle'). He urged students to map Mansfield Park and Dr. Jekyll's London. He rated Tolstoy as great, Turgenev as 'pleasant,' and Dostoevsky as 'mediocre' (praising only The Double). He termed Austen's technique with Fanny Price's emotions the 'knight's move.'

Biographically, Nabokov was a lepidopterist, chess enthusiast, and synesthete. He taught at Cornell (where Thomas Pynchon was his student) and Wellesley. His wife Vera handled academic duties and edited his works. Roman Jakobson resisted his Harvard appointment, saying 'are we next to invite an elephant to be Professor of Zoology?' He published early works under the pen name V. Sirin. His favorite word was 'mauve.' He inspired postmodern writers including Don DeLillo and John Barth, and W.G. Sebald was influenced by him. Phrases from his works appear in the replicant baseline tests in Blade Runner 2049.

Author: this author wrote 'Notes on Prosody' as an appendix to his translation of Eugene Onegin, analyzing Russian and English verse
Work: this memoir opens with 'The cradle rocks above an abyss' and discusses the author's governess Mademoiselle O and an old fat swan
Work: this memoir discusses butterfly-collecting and synesthesia; its last chapter is a second-person address to the author's wife Vera
Work: the title Russian emigre professor at fictional Waindell College boards the wrong train for a lecture in this novel
Work: squirrels appear as portents of misfortune in this novel; the protagonist's former lover Mira Belochkin ('squirrel') died at Buchenwald
Work: in this novel, the title character gives a football to the drawing savant Victor and is confused between football and soccer
Work: a boy suffers from 'referential mania,' thinking everything in the world relates to him, in this short story
Work: this short story ends with a phone ominously ringing a third time after two misdials from a girl asking for Charlie
Work: the final paragraph of this story contains an acrostic spelling a message from the ghosts of Cynthia and Sybil involving icicles and a parking meter
Work: the author had to explain the hidden acrostic in this story to the editor of The New Yorker after they rejected it
Work: narrator Victor recalls a love affair with Nina at a Mediterranean resort; circus posters prefigure her death in a car crash in this story
Work: chess player Luzhin, mentored by Valentinov, plays a disastrous match against Italian grandmaster Turati and jumps from a 'frosted window' in this novel
cards.back: The Defense / The Luzhin Defense (Vladimir Nabokov)

cards.front: Work: set on Anti-Terra/Demonia, siblings Van Veen and Ada fall in love in this novel, which parodies Tolstoy's opening: 'all happy families are more or less dissimilar'
Author: in Lectures on Literature, this author included maps of Bloom's Dublin, sketches of Gregor Samsa as a beetle, and argued Samsa was 'merely a big beetle'
Author: this author rated Tolstoy as great, Turgenev as 'pleasant,' and Dostoevsky as 'mediocre' in his lectures, though he praised The Double
Author: this lepidopterist taught at Cornell (where Thomas Pynchon was his student) and at Wellesley
Author: Roman Jakobson resisted this author's Harvard appointment, saying 'are we next to invite an elephant to be Professor of Zoology?'
Author: this author published early works under the pen name V. Sirin
Author: phrases from this author's works like 'interlinked' and 'a tall white fountain played' are used in the replicant baseline tests in Blade Runner 2049
Author: this author's wife Vera handled his proctoring and office hours, and stopped him from burning the Lolita manuscript
Author: this author termed Austen's technique with Fanny Price's emotions the 'knight's move' in his reading of Mansfield Park
Author: this author called Ezra Pound a 'total fake' and Hemingway and Conrad 'writers of books for boys' in Strong Opinions
Work: in this dystopian novel, a land is ruled by the 'Party of the Average Man' with many references to 'the left'

recursive_suggestions: Lolita — very high clue volume; could be its own topic
Pale Fire — very high clue volume; could be its own topic
Pnin — enough detail for a dedicated search
Ada, or Ardor — enough mentions to warrant further search
Eugene Onegin (Pushkin) — frequently linked through Nabokov's translation
